confirmed.title: Magic sentinel host "example.com" encodes hidden cross-module contract
confirmed.detail: perform() guards `baseURL.host != "example.com"` and throws 'No server configured.' The literal is a hidden coupling to the app target's placeholder default `AppPreferences.defaultAPIBaseURL = URL(string: "https://example.com/rest")!` (MuseAmp/Application/AppPreferences.swift:29). Nothing in the package explains why this host is special; the package's own tests dodge the guard by using subdomains (`music.example.com`, `cache.example.com`). If the app ever changes its placeholder URL, this guard silently stops working and users get raw transport errors to example.com instead of the friendly message.
confirmed.suggestion: Hoist the literal to a named constant (e.g. `private static let unconfiguredPlaceholderHost = "example.com"`) documented as matching AppPreferences.defaultAPIBaseURL, or better: expose an explicit `isConfigured`/placeholder API on the package that APIClient uses, so the contract is visible on both sides instead of via a magic string.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified at SubsonicClientKit/Sources/SubsonicClientKit/SubsonicMusicService.swift:152: perform() guards `baseURL.host != "example.com"` and throws a localized "No server configured." error, with zero explanation in the package — no comment, no named constant, and no other occurrence of the literal in package sources. The sentinel's meaning lives entirely in another module: MuseAmp/Application/AppPreferences.swift:29 defines defaultAPIBaseURL = "https://example.com/rest", which is the default threaded through AppEnvironment, AppEnvironment+Bootstrap, and MuseAmpTV/TVAppContext. Grep confirms this guard is the ONLY unconfigured-server gate in the repo (no isConfigured check in APIClient or UI), so changing the app placeholder would silently disable the friendly error, as the finding claims. Corroborating evidence of the hidden contract: package tests deliberately use subdomains (music.example.com in LiveFixtureTests.swift:8, cache.example.com in CacheTests.swift:56) to dodge the exact-host match, and dozens of app-target tests construct APIClient with bare https://example.com, implicitly relying on the guard to suppress network calls — a second undocumented dependent. AGENTS.md does not endorse sentinel hosts; the repo's conventions favor centralized named keys (AppNotificationUserInfoKey, AppPreferences/ConfigurableKit), so the suggested fix (named constant or explicit unconfigured API visible to both sides) matches local style. Severity adjusted to medium: it is a real maintenance trap that misleads readers and can breed a bug, but there is no evidence it has already caused one — both sides currently agree on the literal.

confirmed.title: playback(id:) reimplements the perform() cache pipeline inline
confirmed.detail: Every other public method funnels through the generic perform(_:cache:decode:) pipeline. playback(id:) instead hand-builds the cache key with the same `"\(baseURL.absoluteString)|\(username)|\(...cacheIdentifier)"` format duplicated from perform() line 158, manually checks `playbackCache.freshValue` (line 118), fetches via fetchSubsonicSong with `cache: nil`, and manually calls `playbackCache.setValue` (line 137). As a side effect of bypassing the canonical path, playback is the only endpoint that gets no request coalescing, no disk caching, and no stale-on-failure fallback — a behavioral divergence a reader cannot see without diffing the two code paths.
confirmed.suggestion: Route playback through the canonical pipeline: `try await perform(.song(id: id), cache: playbackCache) { (payload: SubsonicSongPayload) in ...build PlaybackInfo... }` (the decode closure can throw and capture self like album/song already do). At minimum, extract a `private func cacheKey(for endpoint: SubsonicEndpoint) -> String` so the key format exists once.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified against SubsonicMusicService.swift: playback(id:) (lines 116-139) hand-duplicates perform()'s cache key format (line 117 vs 158 — byte-identical interpolation), manually checks playbackCache.freshValue, fetches through fetchSubsonicSong (whose only caller is playback, and which passes cache: nil into perform), and manually calls setValue. The behavioral divergence is provable from perform()'s guards: cache == nil disables coalescing (line 177), disk read (164), disk write (203), and stale fallback (213), so playback alone loses all three behaviors while its cache property sits indistinguishably beside the four pipeline-backed caches. Refutations failed: disk-caching is raw-payload-based with decode re-run on load, so the auth-bearing playback URL would not be persisted by the suggested fix; the .stream-vs-.song key distinction is unnecessary because caches are separate typed instances; git history (single init commit) shows no deliberate design decision. AGENTS.md says nothing endorsing the pattern, and the dominant module convention is the perform() funnel used by all five other public methods — the suggestion increases conformance with local style. Medium severity is honest: real maintenance seam plus a reader-invisible behavioral inconsistency (playback fails hard offline while album/song serve stale data), but no demonstrated shipped bug.

confirmed.title: perform() duplicates its decode→cache→return tail in the auth-fallback branch
confirmed.detail: The auth-fallback catch branch (lines 195-200: `let mapped = try await decode(fallbackPayload)`, `cache.setValue`, `storeToDisk`, `return mapped`) is a copy of the main success tail (lines 202-207) differing only in which Data it uses. Combined with the do/catch nested inside a do/catch and the redundant if/else at lines 173-178 (`if cache != nil { fetchNetworkData(..., coalescingKey: cacheKey) } else { fetchNetworkData(..., coalescingKey: nil) }`), the 80-line function mixes orchestration with two copies of the same finishing sequence, so a future change (e.g. cache write ordering) must be made in two places.
confirmed.suggestion: Extract a helper like `private func mapAndCache<Response, Output>(_ payload: Response, rawData: Data, cache: ResponseCache<Output>?, cacheKey: String, decode: ...) async throws -> Output` used by both the primary and fallback paths, and collapse lines 173-178 to `let coalescingKey = cache != nil ? cacheKey : nil` followed by a single fetchNetworkData call.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed verbatim in SubsonicMusicService.swift: the auth-fallback catch (lines 195-200) and the main success tail (lines 202-207) are identical decode→cache.setValue→storeToDisk→return sequences differing only in which payload/Data they use, and lines 173-178 are an if/else whose branches differ only in coalescingKey vs nil. The 80-line perform() (147-226) nests do/catch inside do/catch with the inner catch returning rather than rethrowing, making the duplicated tail a second, easy-to-miss exit. The suggested fix matches the file's own dominant style — staleFallback (lines 435-456) is already an extracted generic tail helper taking the same @escaping @Sendable decode closure — so the refactor would read like the rest of the module, and collapsing 173-178 to a single ternary-derived coalescingKey is strictly simpler. AGENTS.md (byte-identical to project CLAUDE.md) contains nothing endorsing duplicated finishing sequences; its style rules favor early returns, responsibility splits, and shared helpers. Severity stays medium because the duplicate copy lives in the rarely-exercised plain-auth fallback branch (only hit when a server rejects token auth), so a change applied only to the hot-path tail (cache write ordering, logging, envelope semantics) would silently diverge without being caught in normal testing.

confirmed.title: loadFreshDataFromDisk and loadStaleDataFromDisk are near-identical copies
confirmed.detail: loadStaleDataFromDisk (lines 409-422) repeats loadFreshDataFromDisk (lines 382-399) verbatim — provider guard, envelope load, version check with remove — except for one TTL guard at line 394. And because `cacheTTL` is the constant `.infinity` (line 18), that TTL guard (`Date().timeIntervalSince(envelope.cachedAt) < cacheTTL`) can never fail, so the two 15-line functions are currently behaviorally identical; the fresh/stale naming promises a distinction that does not exist on the disk path.
confirmed.suggestion: Extract one `private func validCachedEnvelope(forKey:) async -> CacheEnvelope?` that handles the provider guard and version check/removal, then keep the freshness comparison as a one-line call-site difference — or delete the dead TTL plumbing entirely while cacheTTL stays .infinity.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified exactly at the cited lines: loadFreshDataFromDisk (382-399) and loadStaleDataFromDisk (409-422) are verbatim copies except the TTL guard at 394-396, and cacheTTL is the constant .infinity (line 18), making the guard unfailable — the functions are currently behaviorally identical. The duplication includes a side-effecting version-mismatch removal (provider.remove), the kind of copied logic that diverges silently under maintenance. The fresh/stale naming does mirror the in-memory ResponseCache convention (freshValue/staleValue), so the naming-misleads angle is overstated and the finding's secondary suggestion to delete the TTL plumbing should be rejected; but the primary suggestion (extract a shared validCachedEnvelope(forKey:) helper, leaving the TTL comparison as the call-site difference) is a genuine, small clarity gain that matches repo style (guard/early-return) and violates nothing in AGENTS.md. Severity remains low: same-file private duplication, no bug bred yet.

confirmed.title: KeyedDecodingContainer lossy-decoding extension buried at tail of service file
confirmed.detail: SubsonicMusicService.swift's primary export is the service, but lines 689-740 append `private struct SubsonicPingPayload` and a 50-line internal `extension KeyedDecodingContainer` (decodeLossyString, decodeLossyStringIfPresent, decodeLossyIntIfPresent, decodeLossyInt64IfPresent). Every DTO in Models/Subsonic/ (SubsonicSong.swift, SubsonicAlbum.swift, SubsonicArtist.swift) depends on these helpers, yet a reader of those files has no way to discover the definitions live at the bottom of an unrelated 740-line service file. All other payload wrappers (SubsonicSongPayload, SubsonicAlbumPayload, ...) live in Models/Subsonic/, making SubsonicPingPayload's placement inconsistent too.
confirmed.suggestion: Move the extension to its own file next to its consumers, e.g. `Models/Subsonic/KeyedDecodingContainer+LossyDecoding.swift`, and move SubsonicPingPayload into Models/Subsonic/ alongside its sibling payload wrappers.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: SubsonicMusicService.swift (740 lines) ends with a private SubsonicPingPayload (line 689) and an internal KeyedDecodingContainer extension (lines 691-739) whose four lossy-decoding helpers have zero call sites in the service file itself — all ~30 usages are in Models/Subsonic/SubsonicSong.swift, SubsonicAlbum.swift, and SubsonicArtist.swift. All sibling payload wrappers live in Models/Subsonic/, and AGENTS.md neither mandates nor endorses burying Foundation-type extensions in service files (the app target's convention is dedicated Extension+ClassName.swift files, the opposite pattern). The suggested move to Models/Subsonic/KeyedDecodingContainer+LossyDecoding.swift fits package layout and violates no rule. Two downgrades: (1) the SubsonicPingPayload complaint is weak — it is private and its only consumer is ping() in the same file, so its placement is defensible; (2) "no way to discover the definitions" is overstated — jump-to-definition/grep finds them immediately and any misuse fails at compile time, so the issue cannot mislead about behavior or breed bugs. Real but cosmetic file organization, hence low rather than medium.

confirmed.title: Bare Subsonic error codes 40/41 in auth-fallback predicate
confirmed.detail: shouldFallbackToPlainAuth checks `code == 40 || code == 41` with no indication that these are the Subsonic protocol's 'wrong username or password' (40) and 'token authentication not supported' (41) error codes. A reader must consult the Subsonic spec to understand why exactly these two numbers trigger a downgrade to plain-password auth, while the adjacent HTTP check at line 332 (`statusCode == 401 || statusCode == 403`) at least uses universally known values.
confirmed.suggestion: Name the codes, e.g. `private enum SubsonicErrorCode { static let wrongCredentials = 40; static let tokenAuthUnsupported = 41 }`, and consider hoisting the sniffed keyword list (token/salt/password/authentication/credential) to a named static set.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SubsonicMusicService.swift:320-336 — shouldFallbackToPlainAuth checks bare `code == 40 || code == 41`, which are Subsonic spec codes (40 = wrong username/password, 41 = token auth not supported) requiring spec lookup, unlike the adjacent self-evident 401/403 HTTP check. AGENTS.md does not endorse bare protocol numbers; its 'comments only where they remove real ambiguity' rule favors the fix, and the module already names protocol magic values (SubsonicEndpoint.apiVersion, clientName), so named constants would match local convention. Clarity harm is bounded — the function name and the token/salt/password keyword sniff make 'auth error' inferable, so this only costs a spec lookup when modifying the predicate (notably why code 40, wrong credentials, also triggers a plain-auth retry). Real but cosmetic: severity low.

confirmed.title: Resource-type literals "songs"/"albums"/"artists" repeated across mappers and into the app
confirmed.detail: The `type:` literal is written inline five times in this file alone (lines 592, 625, 646, 661, 676), and the same strings are re-typed in app code that constructs Catalog models (e.g. MuseAmp/Backend/Library/AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:40, MuseAmp/Interface/Browse/Support/AlbumNavigationHelper.swift:47/141, AlbumDetailViewController.swift:78). Because CatalogSong/CatalogAlbum/CatalogArtist include `type` in their == and hash(into:) implementations, a single typo at any construction site silently breaks identity comparison and diffable-snapshot dedup.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a public canonical constant per model, e.g. `public static let resourceType = "songs"` on CatalogSong (likewise for album/artist), use it in the mapping extension, and let app-side constructors reference it instead of re-typing the literal.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: the five inline literals exist at exactly the cited lines in SubsonicMusicService.swift, the app re-types them at all cited sites plus PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.swift:242 and three test sites (~16 total across package, app, and tests), and all three Catalog models include `type` in their == and hash(into:) implementations. The models are never decoded from JSON, so `type` is a pure per-model invariant masquerading as free-form String data — every construction site, including app-side stubs (AlbumDetailViewController.swift:78/92/101, AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:40), must re-type the package's magic string correctly or silently produce instances that fail identity comparison against mapper-produced ones. One consequence is overstated: current diffable item identifiers wrap id strings (AlbumItem.track(id:), SearchItem.song(String)), so today's snapshots would not break from a typo; the risk lives in the models' public Hashable contract rather than in any currently exercised dedup path. That tempers but does not refute the finding — the cross-module stringly-typed identity contract genuinely breeds bugs and misleads readers (a `type: String` property gives no hint of its closed value set), satisfying the medium rubric. AGENTS.md contains no endorsement of inline literals; repo conventions favor centralized constants (AppNotificationUserInfoKey, namespaced notification names), and the suggested per-model static constant is idiomatic, cheap, and reads consistently with the rest of the codebase.

confirmed.title: mapSong hardcodes hasLyrics: true for every song
confirmed.detail: mapSong unconditionally sets `hasLyrics: true, hasTimeSyncedLyrics: false` even though the Subsonic song row carries no lyrics information at all. CatalogSongAttributes models these as `Bool?` precisely so 'unknown' is representable, and no app code currently reads either field (grep finds no consumer of `attributes.hasLyrics`), so the hardcoded `true` is a dishonest value waiting to mislead the first future consumer that trusts it.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass `hasLyrics: nil, hasTimeSyncedLyrics: nil` to honestly model that the search/song endpoints do not report lyric availability; let the lyrics(id:) call be the source of truth.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: mapSong (SubsonicMusicService.swift:604-605) hardcodes hasLyrics: true and hasTimeSyncedLyrics: false even though the decoded SubsonicSong struct contains no lyrics field of any kind — both values are fabricated. CatalogSongAttributes models both as Bool? defaulting to nil, so 'unknown' is the designed representation and the fix is just omitting the arguments. The module's own dominant convention contradicts the flagged pattern: the local-library adapter (MuseAmp/Backend/Library/AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:35) maps hasLyrics honestly from hasEmbeddedLyrics and leaves hasTimeSyncedLyrics nil. AGENTS.md does not endorse the pattern; it names lyrics(id:) as the lyrics method, consistent with the suggestion. The only consumer anywhere is a fixture test (LiveFixtureTests.swift:274, '#expect(song.attributes.hasLyrics == true)') inside a test named 'song maps all fields from live response', which locks in the fabricated value under a name implying it was mapped from the response — strengthening rather than refuting the finding, though the fix requires updating that one assertion. Severity stays low: no app behavior reads either field today, so the dishonest values are latent rather than an active bug.

confirmed.title: Results array seeded by repeating the first song as filler
confirmed.detail: `var results = Array(repeating: indexed[0].element, count: indexed.count)` builds the output buffer by replicating song #0 into every slot before the task-group loop overwrites them. The reader must verify the non-empty guard at line 459, reason that every index is guaranteed to be yielded by the group, and only then conclude the filler can never leak — three steps of inference for what should be a self-evident initialization, and any future early-exit from the group would silently duplicate the first song.
confirmed.suggestion: Initialize from the source of truth: `var results = songs.data` — same indices, already-correct fallback values, zero placeholder reasoning needed.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SubsonicMusicService.swift:475 (enrichSongSearchResponse): the results buffer is seeded with `Array(repeating: indexed[0].element, count: indexed.count)`, replicating song #0 into every slot. Correctness depends on two non-local invariants — the non-empty guard at line 459 (else `indexed[0]` traps) and the guarantee that the task group yields every index (else the filler leaks as a duplicated first song). The suggested `var results = songs.data` is semantically equivalent today (each child task's error fallback is the original song at that index, i.e., exactly what songs.data holds), strictly safer under future modification, and removes both hidden invariants. Not endorsed by AGENTS.md, and not a module convention: this is the only Array(repeating:) placeholder seed in the repo; the three other withTaskGroup sites (PlaylistStore.swift:191, PlaybackController+Resolution.swift:68, PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.swift:67) all collect into empty containers. No current bug exists (guard holds, all indices yielded), so severity stays low — cosmetic clarity with latent fragility.

confirmed.title: search() artwork builder uses weak-self/baseURL capture dance unlike sibling closures
confirmed.detail: The search decode closure builds artwork via `{ [serviceBaseURL = self.baseURL, weak self] coverArtID in self?.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID: coverArtID, baseURL: serviceBaseURL) }`, while the album() and song() decode closures (lines 97, 110) simply call `self.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID:)` with a strong capture. Since `baseURL` is a `let` on the class, `serviceBaseURL` is always identical to `self.baseURL`, so the `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter on artworkTemplateURL (line 297) exists solely for this one call site and never changes behavior; the weak capture additionally means artwork silently becomes nil if the service deallocates mid-decode — a divergence from siblings with no payoff.
confirmed.suggestion: Capture self strongly and call `self.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID: $0)` like album()/song() do, then delete the now-unused `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter from artworkTemplateURL.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified in SubsonicMusicService.swift: line 77-79 uses the [serviceBaseURL = self.baseURL, weak self] dance while sibling album()/song()/playback() decode paths (lines 97, 110, 125) use plain strong self. baseURL is `public let` (line 11), so serviceBaseURL always equals self.baseURL; artworkTemplateURL is private and line 78 is the only caller passing baseURL:, making the `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter (line 297) dead weight. The weak capture is also provably inert: the inner capture list evaluates self.baseURL, so the outer decode closure strongly retains self, and artworkBuilder runs synchronously inside perform() — self can never be nil at invocation. AGENTS.md only mandates [weak self] for Combine .sink closures touching UI, which does not apply here; the dominant local convention is strong capture, so the suggested fix increases consistency without violating any rule. The finding slightly overstates the nil-artwork risk (it cannot actually fire), but the core complaint — needless divergence plus a single-call-site dead parameter — is accurate and the fix is behavior-preserving. Severity is correctly low: cosmetic clarity, no behavioral impact.

confirmed.title: Payload decoded even when Subsonic status is not ok
confirmed.detail: init(from:) decodes status and error, then unconditionally runs `payload = try Payload(from: nestedDecoder)`. This only works today because every payload wrapper happens to have exclusively optional/defaulted fields; the moment any payload gains a required field, error responses (status "failed") will throw a keyNotFound DecodingError from this line before decodeSubsonicResponse can inspect `status`, masking the server's actual error message behind a misleading 'Failed to decode' APIError.decodingFailed. The invariant 'all payload fields must be optional' is enforced nowhere and visible nowhere.
confirmed.suggestion: Decode the payload only on success: `payload = status == "ok" ? try Payload(from: nestedDecoder) : nil`, so error envelopes can never be shadowed by payload-shape mismatches.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed verbatim at SubsonicResponse.swift:30 — payload is decoded unconditionally after status/error. The claimed invariant is exactly right: SubsonicAlbumPayload/SubsonicSongPayload/SubsonicSearchResultPayload use optional fields and SubsonicLyricsPayload's custom init never throws, so failed envelopes only decode today by accident of payload shape. decodeSubsonicResponse (SubsonicMusicService.swift:245-271) checks wrapped.status only after decode returns and wraps any DecodingError as APIError.decodingFailed, so a required field in a future payload would mask the server's real error message — and additionally break shouldFallbackToPlainAuth (line 320), which depends on subsonicRequestFailed code 40/41 and message text to trigger the token→plain auth fallback. AGENTS.md does not endorse the pattern; its 'avoid unnecessary optionals' Property Rule actively steers maintainers toward writing the non-optional payload field that springs the trap. The suggested one-line guard matches the file's existing custom-init style and violates no repo convention. Medium severity: behavior is correct today (not high), but the invisible load-bearing invariant misleads readers and is primed to breed a hard-to-diagnose bug.

confirmed.title: Stale window 7 * 24 * 3600 inlined as a default parameter
confirmed.detail: `staleValue(forKey:maxAge: TimeInterval = 7 * 24 * 3600)` hides the package-wide stale-fallback policy (7 days) inside a default-parameter arithmetic expression. The only production caller (SubsonicMusicService.staleFallback line 442) relies on this default, so the single most important cache policy number in the package has no name and no discoverable home.
confirmed.suggestion: Hoist to a named constant, e.g. `static let defaultStaleMaxAge: TimeInterval = 7 * 24 * 3600` on ResponseCache, and use it as the default parameter value.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at /Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/SubsonicClientKit/Sources/SubsonicClientKit/ResponseCache.swift:25 — the 7-day stale window is inlined as a default-parameter expression, and the only production caller (SubsonicMusicService.swift:442, staleFallback) relies on the default, so the policy is invisible at the call site. AGENTS.md does not endorse inline magic time values, and the module's own convention contradicts the pattern: the companion fresh-cache policy is a named property (`private let cacheTTL: TimeInterval = .infinity`, SubsonicMusicService.swift:18). Hoisting to a named constant matches local style. Severity stays low: `7 * 24 * 3600` labeled `maxAge` is readable as 7 days, so the issue is discoverability, not active confusion — though the buried constant does obscure that the parallel disk stale path (loadStaleDataFromDisk) applies no age bound at all. The finding's "single most important number in the package" framing is mildly overstated but the core claim survives adversarial review.

confirmed.title: CatalogArtist omits Identifiable unlike its sibling catalog models
confirmed.detail: CatalogSong (CatalogSong.swift:10) and CatalogAlbum (CatalogAlbum.swift:10) both declare `Decodable, Hashable, Sendable, Identifiable` with identical id-based ==/hash semantics; CatalogArtist declares only `Decodable, Hashable, Sendable` despite having the same `id: String` property and the same identity-based equality. The asymmetry forces callers to special-case artists in any generic Identifiable-constrained code and reads as an oversight rather than a decision.
confirmed.suggestion: Add `Identifiable` to CatalogArtist's conformance list to match CatalogSong and CatalogAlbum.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at CatalogArtist.swift:10 — it declares only Decodable, Hashable, Sendable, while CatalogSong.swift:10 and CatalogAlbum.swift:10 (same author, same creation date, otherwise structurally identical: id+type+href+attributes with the same id+type ==/hash) both add Identifiable. The dominant module convention (2 of 3 sibling catalog resource models) is to conform, so the omission is the deviation and reads as an oversight; adding Identifiable is a zero-cost one-word change auto-satisfied by the existing `id: String`. AGENTS.md contains no rule for or against catalog-model Identifiable conformance (its only Identifiable mention, PlayerItem, conforms), so conventionEndorsed=false. The finding's claim that callers are 'forced to special-case artists' is hypothetical — no current usage (ResourceList containers, relationship fields, SubsonicMusicService mapping) is Identifiable-constrained — so impact is purely cosmetic consistency, keeping severity at low.

confirmed.title: Unexplained size-query filtering inside resolvedURLString
confirmed.detail: After substituting {w}/{h} via string replacement, resolvedURLString re-parses the URL and drops `size` query items whose value is literally "{w}" or "{h}". This looks unreachable — the placeholders were just replaced — and only the test fixture (SubsonicClientKitTests.swift:255, `size=%7Bw%7D`) reveals the actual reason: percent-encoded placeholders survive the raw string replacement and only appear as "{w}" once URLComponents decodes the query. The function thus mixes high-level template resolution with a subtle encoding-edge-case repair, with zero in-code explanation of why the second pass exists.
confirmed.suggestion: Extract the filter into a named helper such as `removingUnresolvedSizePlaceholders(_:)`, or add a one-line comment stating that percent-encoded {w}/{h} placeholders bypass the string replacement and must be stripped after URL parsing.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified in Artwork.swift lines 26-44: after literal {w}/{h} replacement, the function filters `size` query items whose decoded value is "{w}"/"{h}". This filter appears unreachable to a reader since the placeholders were just replaced, but URLComponents percent-decodes query values, so encoded `size=%7Bw%7D` (test fixture SubsonicClientKitTests.swift:255) bypasses the string replacement and only surfaces as "{w}" post-parse; the `.lowercased()` also silently handles case variants. No comment or named helper explains either subtlety — intent is documented only in the test file. AGENTS.md does not endorse the unexplained pattern; its comment rule ("only where they remove real ambiguity") explicitly permits the suggested one-line comment, and a small named helper matches repo style. The suggestion improves clarity without violating conventions. Severity stays low: a maintainer deleting the seemingly-dead filter would be caught by the dedicated unit test, so it is a genuine but cosmetic clarity gap rather than an active bug breeder.

confirmed.title: Transition-publish sequence duplicated across loadAndPlay, continueWithCurrentEngineItem, and restorePlayback
confirmed.detail: The ~20-line tail that publishes a track transition is copy-pasted three times: loadAndPlay lines 109-131, continueWithCurrentEngineItem lines 170-192, and restorePlayback (MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift lines 58-89). Each repeats the identical sequence: engine.play()/mediaCenterCoordinator.activateSessionIfPossible()/setState(.playing), nowPlayingManager.setTrack + updateRate, snapshot capture, nowPlayingManager.updateQueueInfo(index: snap.history.count, count: snap.totalCount), remoteCommandManager.updateEnabledCommands, the 4-argument updateLikeCommand call, and the didTransitionTo/didChangeQueue delegate pair. Any change to the now-playing publishing contract (e.g. a new media-center field) must be replicated in three files, and the three copies have already drifted slightly (restorePlayback orders setTrack before play, the others after).
confirmed.suggestion: Extract one internal helper on MusicPlayer, e.g. publishTransition(to item: PlayerItem, reason: TransitionReason, startPlaying: Bool), that owns the setTrack/updateRate/updateQueueInfo/updateEnabledCommands/updateLikeCommand/delegate sequence, and call it from all three sites. This fits the existing responsibility-based extension split (it can live in MusicPlayer+Playback.swift).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: loadAndPlay (MusicPlayer+Playback.swift:109-131) and continueWithCurrentEngineItem (170-192) share a literally identical ~22-line transition-publish tail (play/activate/setState, setTrack/updateRate, snapshot, updateQueueInfo, updateEnabledCommands, 4-arg updateLikeCommand, didTransitionTo+didChangeQueue). restorePlayback (MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift:50-90) repeats the same contract a third time with autoPlay branching; the 4-arg updateLikeCommand block additionally appears verbatim in configureLikeCommand and setCurrentItemLiked (MusicPlayer.swift:203-208, 220-225). AGENTS.md does not endorse the duplication — its conventions (shared helpers over per-file ad-hoc code, responsibility-based MusicPlayer+Xxx.swift extensions) favor the suggested internal publishTransition helper, which would read like the rest of the module. The finding overstates two points, justifying a downgrade from high to medium: (1) the three copies are not all identical — restorePlayback's setTrack-before-play ordering is plausibly deliberate (queue info must be set before the await-seek, play comes after seek), so the cited "drift" is not evidence of an existing bug; (2) no actual bug has been demonstrated, only the risk that a contract change (e.g. new media-center field) must be replicated across sites. That risk is real and breeds bugs (medium), but nothing is currently broken or blocked (not high).

confirmed.title: Item-failure handling (NSError construction + advance-or-stop) duplicated in two files
confirmed.detail: continueWithCurrentEngineItem lines 144-165 and observeItemStatus in MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 91-113 contain the same block twice: build NSError(domain: "MuseAmpPlayerKit", code: -1, userInfo: [NSLocalizedDescriptionKey: String(localized: "Unknown playback error", bundle: .module)]) from avItem.error, fire delegate didFailItem, then either loadAndPlay(playbackQueue.advance(), reason: .itemFailed) or musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue + stop(). The error domain string, magic code -1, and localized fallback message are inlined at both sites; the recovery policy can silently drift between the preloaded-item path and the KVO path.
confirmed.suggestion: Add an internal helper, e.g. handleItemFailure(_ item: PlayerItem, underlying: Error?), that builds the fallback NSError (with the domain/code hoisted to named constants) and performs the didFailItem + advance-or-stop logic; call it from both continueWithCurrentEngineItem and observeItemStatus.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified verbatim: MusicPlayer+Playback.swift lines 145-165 (continueWithCurrentEngineItem) and MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 91-113 (observeItemStatus) each inline the same fallback NSError(domain: "MuseAmpPlayerKit", code: -1, userInfo: [NSLocalizedDescriptionKey: String(localized: "Unknown playback error", bundle: .module)]) and the same recovery policy (didFailItem -> playbackQueue.advance() -> loadAndPlay(.itemFailed) or musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue + stop()). Grep confirms these are the only two occurrences of the domain string and localized fallback in the package, with no existing shared helper. The only divergence between the copies is logging (the preloaded path never logs the error at .error level while the KVO path does), which is incidental drift rather than deliberate policy difference — exactly the risk the finding describes. AGENTS.md (identical to project CLAUDE.md) contains no endorsement of inlined duplicated error handling; it actually favors shared helpers over per-file ad-hoc duplication. The suggested internal handleItemFailure helper matches the module's existing style (loadAndPlay, preloadNextItem, continueWithCurrentEngineItem are already internal cross-file helpers in MusicPlayer extensions), so the fix would read like the rest of the repo. Severity stays medium: the duplicated block is the playback engine's failure-recovery policy, where one-sided edits (retry logic, failure-loop fixes, end-of-queue changes) would silently diverge between the gapless/preloaded path and the KVO path.

confirmed.title: End-of-queue sequence (didReachEndOfQueue + stop) duplicated at five sites
confirmed.detail: The pair delegate?.musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue(self) followed by stop() is repeated in MusicPlayer+Playback.swift lines 95-96 (loadAndPlay nil guard) and 161-163, MusicPlayer+Queue.swift lines 21-22 (next), and MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 77-80 (handleItemEnd, conditionally) and 110-112 (observeItemStatus). If the end-of-queue contract changes (e.g. state should become .idle without clearing the queue), five sites must be found and edited.
confirmed.suggestion: Extract an internal helper, e.g. func endQueuePlayback(), encapsulating the delegate notification plus stop(), and call it from all five sites (handleItemEnd can pass whether stop() applies, or keep its repeatMode check around the call).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified all five cited sites exist and contain the delegate?.musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue(self) + stop() sequence: MusicPlayer+Playback.swift:95-96 and 161-162, MusicPlayer+Queue.swift:21-22, MusicPlayer+Observation.swift:77-80 (stop conditional on repeatMode == .off) and 110-111. A grep confirms these are the only five emit sites, matching the finding exactly, including its caveat that handleItemEnd is conditional. AGENTS.md does not endorse the duplication; the module already centralizes shared playback transitions in internal helpers (loadAndPlay, continueWithCurrentEngineItem, preloadNextItem) under a MARK: Internal section, so an extracted endQueuePlayback() helper would match local style rather than diverge from it. The hazard is real but mild: the sequence is only two statements, behavior is currently consistent across sites, and the one divergence (no stop() when repeat-queue exhausts) is intentional and would need a parameter in the helper. Cosmetic clarity/maintenance seam, no evidence it has bred a bug.

confirmed.title: Dead private method rebuildShufflePermutationIndices with a factually false comment
confirmed.detail: private mutating func rebuildShufflePermutationIndices() (lines 482-488) is never called (verified by grep). Its comment states "This is called after clearUpcoming which removes items." — but clearUpcoming (lines 329-357) never calls it; clearUpcoming does its own indexMap-based rebuild inline. A reader auditing shuffle-index correctness will waste time reconciling this phantom step with the real flow.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete rebuildShufflePermutationIndices entirely (the inline rebuild in clearUpcoming is the real implementation), or if it is meant to be the canonical rebuild, call it from clearUpcoming and remove the inline duplicate.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: rebuildShufflePermutationIndices (PlaybackQueue.swift lines 482-488) has zero call sites repo-wide (grep finds only the definition; it is private/file-scoped and mutating on a struct, so no dynamic dispatch is possible). Its comment "This is called after clearUpcoming which removes items" is factually false — clearUpcoming (lines 329-357) does its own indexMap-based rebuild inline and never calls it. Worse, the dead method's filter-only body does not perform the contiguous-index remapping its own comment describes, so reviving it per the comment would introduce a stale-index bug. AGENTS.md does not endorse the pattern; its "keep comments rare and only where they remove real ambiguity" rule supports deletion. The suggested fix (delete the dead method) improves clarity and matches repo conventions.

refuted.title: Per-song enrichment failures vanish with no trace
refuted.reason: The code exists as cited (SubsonicMusicService.swift:464-473 silently falls back to the thin search row on per-song enrichment failure), but the finding does not hold up. (1) The enrichment path is dead from the app's perspective: both APIClient callers (APIClient.swift:74, :95) and all five test call sites pass prefetchSongMetadata: false, so enrichSongSearchResponse is never invoked by any in-repo consumer — the claimed 'APIClient cannot observe degradation' scenario cannot occur because APIClient deliberately opts out of enrichment. (2) AGENTS.md's swallowed-error logging rule is explicitly scoped to app code via AppLog (MuseAmp/Backend/Logging/AppLog.swift), which the package cannot import; the dominant deliberate convention of SubsonicClientKit is uniform log-free best-effort degradation (silent staleFallback, silent token→plain auth fallback, try? URL/decode fallbacks) with zero diagnostics seams anywhere in the package. (3) The suggested init-injected error handler would be the package's only such seam, diverging from the SubsonicMusicService(baseURL:username:password:) init documented in AGENTS.md's Package Reference, adding API surface for a path nothing exercises — the fix would make the code read unlike the rest of the module. The fallback-to-search-row is the intended best-effort semantic of metadata prefetching, not an unhandled error condition.

scopeSummaries.summary: This scope is the iOS app's bootstrap chain and adaptive root shell. Entry is manual: main.swift wraps UIApplicationMain in MainActor.assumeIsolated -> AppDelegate (minimal: AlertController theming, scene config, Mac Catalyst Cmd+W/Q interception with a quit-confirmation gate driven by the pure-function MacCatalystTerminationPolicy, background-URLSession completion-handler storage, and a global terminateApplication() -> Never) -> SceneDelegate, which shows BootProgressController as the initial root, builds AppEnvironment off-main (DatabaseManager.initializeSynchronously inside a Task), then cross-fades to MainController and triggers playback restoration plus downloadManager.reconcileOnLaunch(). AppEnvironment is the composition root and sole owner of all backend services (~18 stored lets: APIClient, DatabaseManager, MusicLibraryDatabase, PlaylistStore, DownloadStore, DownloadManager, PlaybackController, lyrics services, AudioFileImporter, etc.), with responsibility-split extensions: +Bootstrap (static DatabaseManager factory injecting RuntimeDependencies closures across the app->MuseAmpDatabaseKit boundary, AppLog sink bridging, Kingfisher cache config), +Events (Combine subscription to databaseManager.events that fans out as amusic.* NotificationCenter posts and refreshes the global TrackTitleSanitizer), and +Transfer (SyncTransferSession factory). MainController is the adaptive root: it computes LayoutMode (.compact/.relaxed/.catalyst) from trait/layout passes and swaps between a child TabBarController (compact: UITab on iOS 18+, legacy viewControllers on 16-17, conditional Search tab keyed off AppPreferences.currentSubsonicConfiguration) and a PopupBarSplitViewController hosting SidebarViewController plus cached per-destination/per-playlist UINavigationControllers. The LNPopupController "Now Playing" bar is managed twice in deliberately duplicated code (TabBarController+Popup and MainController+Popup) binding PlaybackController $snapshot/playbackTimeSubject, with PopupBarPagingHandler translating popup-bar swipes into skipToQueueTrack via LNPopupItem.userInfo queue indexes under a 0.5s cooldown. SceneDelegate also owns all external data entry: file-URL imports (audio extension whitelist, .museampplaylist, .subsonicconfig) coalesced over 200ms and queued until MainController exists, plus museamp://tv URLs carrying base64-JSON SyncReceiverHandshakeInfo with protocol-version gating. Extension/ holds five tiny Extension+ClassName.swift files (Bundle.appIcon, String.sanitizedTrackTitle delegating to TrackTitleSanitizer, UIColor.shiftedHue, UITableView header/footer autosizing, UIView.removeAnimationsRecursively). AppPreferences is a nonisolated static enum facade over ConfigurableKit/UserDefaults holding Subsonic server URL normalization (forces https + /rest suffix), credentials, and sort-option persistence.

Backend/API holds APIClient, the single app-level network orchestration entry point: a final @unchecked Sendable class wrapping SubsonicClientKit's SubsonicMusicService behind intent-level async methods (ping, searchSongs/searchAlbums, album, song, lyrics, playback) plus artwork URL template resolution (resolveMediaURL handles {w}/{h} placeholders, protocol-relative //, scheme-less relative URLs against baseURL). Its mutable configuration (baseURL/username/password) is NSLock-guarded and lazily re-read from AppPreferences.currentSubsonicConfiguration on every synchronizedState() call, hot-rebuilding the underlying service when server settings change; APIClient+Transfer.swift extends it with the LAN sync-transfer HTTP surface (POST /auth bearer-token handshake, GET /manifest with protocol-version gate, GET /track/{id} download with cleanup-on-failure) speaking Backend/Sync JSON models. Backend/Library is the thin bridge between app code and MuseAmpDatabaseKit's DatabaseManager: MusicLibraryDatabase (core + +Tracks/+Downloads/+Ingest/+Bridge extensions) routes reads through synchronous DatabaseManager queries and writes through LibraryCommand (sendSynchronouslyIfSupported first, async send fallback); +Bridge.swift is currently an empty placeholder. AudioFileImporter is the file-picker import pipeline: security-scoped access held for the whole batch, recursive audio discovery, AVAsset metadata inspection, mandatory catalog trackID/albumID extraction from embedded JSON comment (files without it are rejected as noMetadata), three-layer duplicate detection (intra-batch set, existing DB records via case-insensitive title/artist/album + 2s duration tolerance, destination-file existence), staging copy into paths.incomingDirectory, then database.ingestAudioFile to commit, with optional detached-Task background artwork fetch. EmbeddedMetadataReader extracts AVFoundation metadata (title/artist/album/track/disc/genre/composer/releaseDate/lyrics/artwork presence) into AudioTrackRecord values with localized Unknown Artist/Album fallbacks. AudioTrackRecord+AppModels is the adapter seam converting DB records into PlaylistEntry, CatalogSong, PlaybackTrack, SongExportItem, and ImportedTrackMetadata for the Playback/Playlist/Sync/Interface layers. SongLibraryIndexer wraps the .rebuildIndex command; MusicLibraryTrackRemovalService loops .removeTrack synchronously (swallowing errors after logging); TrackArtworkRepairService re-resolves artwork (embedded comment JSON, then song lookup, then album lookup via APIClient at 600x600), caches it atomically, embeds it into the audio file via DownloadArtworkProcessor, and posts .artworkDidUpdate. State ownership: AppEnvironment constructs all these as let properties; persisted state lives in DatabaseManager (WCDB inside MuseAmpDatabaseKit); this scope keeps no caches beyond APIClient's lock-guarded config.

This scope owns the entire download pipeline for remote songs: queueing, URL resolution, transfer via the Digger library, network-aware deferral, post-download finalization (artwork/lyrics/metadata embedding), library ingestion, and crash-safe persistence. The central type is `DownloadManager`, a `@MainActor` class split across five files (core + `+Queue`, `+Digger`, `+Network`, `+Persistence` extensions); it owns all in-memory download state in a `tasks: [String: ActiveDownloadTask]` dictionary keyed by trackID, exposes it to UI via a Combine `CurrentValueSubject` (`tasksPublisher`), and is constructed once in `AppEnvironment` (no singleton). `DownloadStore` is a thin, non-throwing facade over `MusicLibraryDatabase` (MuseAmpDatabaseKit/WCDB) that persists `DownloadJob` records for in-flight work only — completed downloads delete their record and the track lands in the library tracks table; "is downloaded" is derived from the tracks table plus on-disk file existence, never from a stored flag. The task lifecycle is a state machine (`waiting -> resolving -> downloading -> finalizing`, with `waitingForNetwork`, `paused`, `failed` branches), with every state transition mirrored to the database via `persistRecord` so that `reconcileOnLaunch` (called from SceneDelegate) can rehydrate interrupted/queued/finalizing/failed jobs after a relaunch. Finalization moves the Digger cache file to a `.tmp.`-prefixed staging file, concurrently fetches artwork (`DownloadArtworkProcessor`) and lyrics (`DownloadLyricsProcessor`), embeds metadata via `ExportMetadataProcessor` (AVAssetExportSession passthrough re-export including a JSON comment carrying trackID/albumID), then hands the file to `DatabaseManager.send(.ingestAudioFile)` for library ingestion. `NetworkMonitor` (NWPathMonitor wrapper) drives WiFi/cellular/none transitions; cellular downloads are deferred per-track unless explicitly allowed via `allowCellularDownload`. `ExportMetadataProcessor` is also reused outside this scope by `Backend/Lyrics/LyricsReloadService` and `Backend/Sync/SyncPreparedTrackBuilder+Export`, so its embed/verify contract crosses feature boundaries.

MuseAmp/Backend/Playback owns all app-level playback state. The central type is PlaybackController (@MainActor final class, ObservableObject, 553 lines plus four responsibility extensions), which wraps MuseAmpPlayerKit.MusicPlayer, acts as its MusicPlayerDelegate, and is the single source of truth UI observes via the @Published `snapshot` (PlaybackSnapshot) and the high-frequency `playbackTimeSubject`. It is created once in AppEnvironment (iOS) and TVAppContext (tvOS) — every Playback file is mirrored into MuseAmpTV/Backend/Playback via relative symlinks, so this scope compiles in both targets. Internal mutable state is grouped into nested nonisolated structs (QueueState with currentSource/trackLookup/itemCache, RestoreState, SeekState). Supporting value types are PlaybackTrack (the app's canonical track model), PlaybackSnapshot (immutable derived view with computed currentTrack/history/upcoming), PlaybackSource (custom-Codable enum tagging where a queue came from), PlaybackOutputDevice (AVAudioSession route mapping), and PlaybackSessionStore (JSON file persistence of PersistedPlaybackSession/PersistedPlaybackTrack for queue restore across launches). PlaybackController+Resolution.swift is the local-file-only URL resolution layer: it maps PlaybackTracks to PlayerItems by probing track.localFileURL, then database.allTrackRelativePaths() under paths.audioDirectory, then a cached item, and throws PlaybackResolutionError.localFileUnavailable otherwise — there is no remote streaming fallback. Queue PlayerItems get synthetic IDs ("queue-item|UUID|trackID") built/parsed by makeQueueItemID/sourceTrackID so duplicate tracks can coexist in a queue. MuseAmp/Backend/Models is a thin adapter layer: five small extension files that convert between SubsonicClientKit's CatalogSong, MuseAmpDatabaseKit's PlaylistEntry/AudioTrackRecord, PlaybackTrack, and MuseAmpInterfaceKit cell-content structs (SongRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AMNowPlayingQueueTrackPresenting), plus SongExportItem, the value type carrying source URL + sanitized "Artist - Title" base filename for export/share/sync flows (not mirrored to tvOS, which has its own TV* models).

This scope holds two small app-target backend subdomains, both constructed and owned by AppEnvironment (MuseAmp/Application/AppEnvironment.swift) and both delegating actual persistence to MuseAmpDatabaseKit. The Playlist side centers on PlaylistStore, a non-throwing facade over DatabaseManager: every mutation (create/import/delete/rename/cover/addSong/removeSong/move/duplicate/clear/updateLyrics) is expressed as a LibraryCommand sent via databaseManager.sendSynchronously, after which the store re-fetches its in-memory `playlists: [Playlist]` cache and posts `.playlistsDidChange` ("amusic.playlistsDidChange") only when an Equatable diff against the pre-mutation snapshot shows change. It special-cases a sentinel zero-UUID "Liked Songs" playlist (Playlist.swift extension): auto-created at init with a generated heart PNG (LikedSongsPlaylistArtwork), name re-synced to the current locale, metadata backfilled, and auto-deleted when its last song is removed. PlaylistCoverArtworkCache is an actor that renders NxN mosaic covers from entry artwork via Kingfisher, caching in an NSCache plus PNG files under Caches/PlaylistCoverArtworkCache keyed by "v3-{id}-{updatedAt ms}-{songCount}-{sidePixels}", and posting `.playlistArtworkDidUpdate`. PlaylistTransferDocument is the versioned Codable .musiclist export/import format (drops entryID/artworkURL/lyrics; tolerates a legacy `entries` JSON key). The Lyrics side has LyricsService (cache-first read: LyricsCacheStore, then APIClient.lyrics, persisting only for locally downloaded tracks), LyricsReloadService (heavier path: prefer lyrics embedded in the downloaded M4A via AVFoundation, else fetch remote, persist to cache, re-embed into the audio file via ExportMetadataProcessor, post `.lyricsDidUpdate`; also a destructive rebuildAllLyricsIndex that wipes the whole cache then refetches every track sequentially), and LyricsChineseScriptConverter, a pure CFStringTransform Hans/Hant presentation utility with no persistence. UI layers (Interface/Playlist, Interface/NowPlaying, Interface/Sync, Interface/Root) consume these services through AppEnvironment and observe via NotificationCenter and the DatabaseKit events publisher.

This scope holds four backend subdomains of a pure-UIKit iOS/tvOS music app. Backend/Sync implements a complete LAN song-transfer stack: SyncProtocol.swift defines all wire/domain models (version-gated protocol v4 via SyncConstants, SyncEndpoint with IPv6/scope-aware host parsing, SyncConnectionInfo for QR handoff, SyncManifest/SyncManifestEntry, SyncPlaylistSession with expected/unique track-count invariants, SyncTransferError); SyncServer.swift is a hand-rolled HTTP/1.1 server over NWListener (routes POST /auth, GET /manifest, GET /track/{id}, password-to-Bearer-token auth via an NSLock-guarded TokenStore, 1 MiB request cap, 64 KiB chunked file streaming, progress callbacks); SyncTransferSession.swift is the @MainActor orchestrator owning both sender state (password, prepared batch, server, Bonjour advertiser) and receiver state (browser, temp download directory), delegating network client calls to APIClient (APIClient+Transfer.swift) and imports to AudioFileImporter. SyncPreparedTrackBuilder(+Export) prepares per-track temp copies with embedded catalog metadata/lyrics/artwork off the main thread; SyncBonjourAdvertiser/Browser handle NetService publish/discover with role+protocolVersion TXT records; TVPlaylistSessionStore persists and strictly validates a JSON playlist-session manifest on tvOS. Most Sync files are mirrored into MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync via relative symlinks, so they must compile for both targets (tvOS sender path is stubbed to throw). Backend/Logging/AppLog.swift is the single mandated diagnostics facade wrapping Dog with category-name normalization and a Layout sub-channel. Backend/Supplement holds cross-cutting nonisolated utilities: ConcurrencyHelpers (UncheckedSendableBox, OnceGuard, ContinuationOnceGuard, nonAccumulatingTimer, asyncMapLatest), TrackTitleSanitizer (frequency-based trailing-pattern stripping with a lock-guarded static cache refreshed from library titles), ChineseScriptSearchNormalizer/SearchTextMatcher (Hans/Hant search-form expansion and highlight ranges), AVMetadataHelper, and StringUtilities (sanitizedLogText, isCatalogID). Backend/MenuProviders contains injected UIKit menu factories (Song/Playlist/AddToPlaylist/Playback context menus plus stateless Copy/Download/MenuSection/preview helpers) that fetch data at action time through escaping provider closures and mutate state only through PlaylistStore/PlaybackController. State ownership: SyncTransferSession instances are created per-flow by AppEnvironment+Transfer (iOS) and TVAppContext (tvOS); menu providers hold no domain state of their own.

This scope holds the local-library browse UI for the iOS app target: three list screens (SongLibraryViewController for album groups in Browse/Albums, SongsViewController for flat tracks in Browse/Songs, DownloadsViewController for active download tasks in Browse/Downloads) plus the drill-in AlbumDetailViewController in Browse/AlbumDetail and the shared AlbumNavigationHelper in Browse/Support that constructs/pushes album detail screens, including synthesizing a CatalogAlbum from local AudioTrackRecords when the album exists on disk. All lists use UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by lightweight ID enums (LibraryItem/SongsItem/AlbumItem/String trackID) with side dictionaries (albumsByID, tracksByID, searchTracksByID, tasksByTrackID) that cell providers resolve at dequeue time; controllers own only presentation state while all domain state lives behind AppEnvironment services (libraryDatabase/databaseManager, downloadStore/downloadManager, playlistStore, playbackController, lyricsService, musicLibraryTrackRemovalService, audioFileImporter). Interface/Collections is a library of reusable TableBaseCell subclasses (AmSongCell, AmMediaCell/AmMediaRowView, AlbumTrackCell, DownloadProgressCell, PlaylistHeaderCell, skeleton/show-more/search cells) configured exclusively through Hashable value-type *Content structs (SongRowContent, MediaRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AlbumHeaderContent, AMDownloadProgressContent, ArtworkContent), with model-to-content adapter inits living in Backend/Models or in private controller extensions. Interface/Common provides the shared infrastructure: TableBaseCell, MediaDetailViewController base class, EmptyStateView, the Interface animation wrapper + InterfaceStyle/PlatformInterfacePalette style enums, MuseAmpImageView (a Kingfisher-backed image view with manual loadID ticketing, disk-cache decode queue, shimmer, and QuickLook preview), and the Presenters folder of toast/alert/progress/export presenters (SongExportPresenter, PlaybackFeedbackPresenter, DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter, ConfirmationAlertPresenter, ProgressActionPresenter, LyricsReloadPresenter, TrackArtworkRepairPresenter, ImageQuickLookPreviewPresenter). AlbumDetailViewController is the most complex type: it accepts a full album, a stub album, or a bare song/album ID, lazily resolves the real album via APIClient, patches missing artwork from track attributes, and exposes download/playlist/export/playback menus per the album-menu rules. Note for reviewers: the project CLAUDE.md claims TableBaseCell/Interface/EmptyStateView/MediaDetailViewController live in a MuseAmpInterfaceKit package, but no such package exists in this working tree — the files in Interface/Common ARE the canonical implementations here, not shims.

This scope is the full-screen Now Playing experience, presented via LNPopupController (never a tab). Two root controllers exist for the two layout worlds: NowPlayingCompactController (iPhone; hosts NowPlayingCompactPageController, which horizontally pages queue/artwork/lyrics via NowPlayingPagingSectionView) and NowPlayingRelaxedController (iPad/Catalyst; NowPlayingRelaxedShellView with left artwork+transport panel and a right panel toggling lyrics/queue via a segmented control). Both controllers are thin: nearly all behavior is shared through a tower of @MainActor "shell controller" protocols in Support/ (NowPlayingShellController -> NowPlayingPlaybackShellController -> Queue/Artwork/Lyrics/Lifecycle/Transport variants) whose default implementations in `where Self: UIViewController` extensions do the Combine binding, queue action wiring, popup preparation, and suspension handling — effectively protocol mixins. State is owned upstream by Backend's PlaybackController (reached via AppEnvironment); this layer only mirrors it into a locally cached `currentPlaybackSnapshot` and derives presentation through NowPlayingControlIslandViewModel + the pure nonisolated NowPlayingContentMapper (PlaybackSnapshot -> AMNowPlayingContent + BackgroundSource + animation flags). Queue rows are built by AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder (history capped at 3, upcoming at 10, occurrence-suffixed IDs for duplicate tracks, footer with remaining count/minutes) and rendered by NowPlayingQueueSectionView, a manual UITableViewDataSource with hand-rolled change detection (NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate) and anchored auto-scroll with a 1s programmatic-scroll block window. Lyrics live in LyricTimeline/: LyricTimelineView owns its own Combine pipeline (track-ID changes + .lyricsDidUpdate notification + playbackTimeSubject -> LyricsPhase -> item Snapshot), parsing LRC via LyricParser/LyricTimeline (offset tags, multi-timestamp lines, binary-search active line). LyricSheet/ holds LyricSelectionSheetViewController, a multi-select copy sheet that is the only diffable-data-source table in the scope. Components/ contains the transport view class hierarchy (base NowPlayingTransportView + compact/relaxed subclasses, split into +Actions/+Layout/+Navigation), self-subscribing PlayerControlButton subclasses, a scrubbing ProgressTrackView, and the ColorfulX-backed animated artwork background whose palette is computed by NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator (DominantColors on a background queue, LRUCache of 32 keyed by artwork URL string). Notably, Support/NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator.swift appears to be unreferenced in the iOS target (LyricTimelineView+Binding now does its own loading; tvOS has its own TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator), making it likely dead code worth a reviewer's eye.

This scope holds four feature-owned UIKit areas of the iOS app target, all programmatic UIKit with no storyboards. Playlist/ contains the playlist library list (PlaylistViewController + Editing/Search/Table extensions, diffable table over PlaylistStore data with sort options, multi-select editing, and local fuzzy search), the playlist detail screen (PlaylistDetailViewController + Actions/Artwork/Menu extensions, with drag-export, reorder/remove via a custom diffable data source subclass, generated/custom cover artwork, and download-all), plus PlaylistTransferCoordinator which imports/exports playlists as JSON documents through document pickers and share sheets. Search/ is a remote-catalog search screen (SearchViewController + Search/Table extensions) backed by a value-type SearchResultsState struct and a UserDefaults-backed SearchHistoryStore, querying APIClient.searchSongs/searchAlbums in parallel with 300 ms Task.sleep debounce, pagination via SearchPage offsets, and result deduplication. Settings/ is a ConfigurableKit-driven StackScrollController (SettingsViewController + API/Actions/Lyrics extensions) covering Subsonic server-profile import/validate/export/delete (ServerProfileImportCoordinator), transfers entry, downloads, lyrics/title tweaks, database and lyrics-index rebuilds, and a self-contained LogViewerController that parses Dog log files. Sync/ is the LAN song-transfer UI: SyncRoleSelectionViewController fans out to a multi-select SyncContentPickerViewController (albums/songs/playlists tabs), sender-side SyncServerStatusViewController and SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController (Bonjour + QR + temporary password), receiver-side SyncReceiverViewController (discovery, QR scan, manual endpoint+password), and SyncTransferProgressViewController which drives the manifest/compare/download/import phases. State ownership follows the project rule: PlaylistStore (Backend) owns playlist data and controllers re-read it on .playlistsDidChange; SyncTransferSession (Backend/Sync, created per-flow via environment.makeSyncTransferSession()) owns transfer state and feeds local Phase/State enums; search and log-viewer state are controller-local value types. All Sync and Settings screens render by fully tearing down and rebuilding StackScrollController arranged subviews (refreshUI pattern), while Playlist/Search use diffable snapshots with hasAppliedInitialSnapshot gating.

MuseAmpTV is a deliberately thin tvOS receiver shell: its only product feature is receiving a playlist from an iPhone over LAN (Bonjour + QR pairing) and playing it back full-screen with lyrics. Entry is main.swift (MainActor.assumeIsolated + UIApplicationMain, no @main) -> TVAppDelegate -> TVSceneDelegate, which boots through TVBootProgressController (synchronous DatabaseManager init off-main, then restoreSessionStateAtLaunch) before crossfading to TVRootViewController. TVAppContext (split into TVAppContext.swift, +Bootstrap, +Session) is the TV analogue of AppEnvironment and owns all shared dependencies: APIClient, MusicLibraryDatabase, PlaylistStore, DownloadStore, PlaybackController, LyricsService, AudioFileImporter, SyncTransferSession, and TVPlaylistSessionStore, plus the in-memory currentSessionManifest and one-shot pending alerts. Almost the entire Backend/ tree (API, Downloads, Library, Logging, Lyrics, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, Sync) plus Application/AppPreferences.swift are relative symlinks into MuseAmp/ — far broader than the Sync-only mirroring AGENTS.md documents — so the only TV-native backend code is Backend/Models/ (four small AMTV-prefixed flow/content types) and Backend/Sync/TVSessionStateAdapter.swift. TVSessionStateAdapter (714 lines) is the central state machine: a private TransferPhase enum (waiting/connecting/receiving/importing/completed/disconnected/failed) is combined with the playback snapshot and session manifest into a computed AMTVLibrarySessionState, which TVRootViewController collapses to AMTVRootFlowState and renders by toggling isHidden on three pre-allocated surfaces (TVQRPairingView, TVTransferProgressView, TVNowPlayingController) with snapshot crossfades and tvOS focus updates. TVNowPlayingController hosts a split artwork/transport + read-only lyric timeline view, observing $snapshot and playbackTimeSubject, with a local TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator and a TV-specific LRC parser/timeline (TVLyricParser, TVLyricTimeline) feeding TVLyricTimelineView. State ownership: transfer phase lives in TVSessionStateAdapter, session manifest + alerts in TVAppContext, playback in the shared PlaybackController; views are stateless renderers driven by a single reloadContent() pass.

MuseAmpDatabaseKit is a Swift 6 SwiftPM package whose only dependency is WCDBSwift (Lakr233/wcdb-spm-prebuilt); it owns the file-backed local music library: two SQLite databases, the on-disk audio tree, artwork/lyrics caches, and audit reporting. The central type is `DatabaseManager` (an `@unchecked Sendable` final class) which composes stateless internal structs — `IndexStore` (library_index.sqlite: the derived, rebuildable track index), `StateStore` (library_state.sqlite: authoritative user state — download_jobs, playlists, playlist_entries, meta tables), `LibraryScanner` (disk-to-index rebuild), `LibraryFileManager` (staged .tmp moves into `Audio/<albumID>/<trackID>.<ext>`), `CacheCoordinator` (artwork .jpg / lyrics .lrc caches plus orphan pruning), `DownloadCoordinator` (download-job rows only; actual transport lives in the app target), and `DatabaseBootstrapper` (opens both stores, stamps schema versions in index_meta/state_meta). The public surface is command/event shaped: callers `send(_: LibraryCommand)` (sync fast paths via `sendSynchronouslyIfSupported`, async paths `rebuildIndex`/`ingestAudioFile` isolated to the `@DatabaseActor` global actor) and observe a Combine `events: AnyPublisher<LibraryEvent, Never>` for change notifications (tracksChanged, downloadsChanged, playlistsChanged, cache-changed, audit). `initialize()` must run exactly once (NSLock + precondition); stores are optionals and every public query calls `requireInitialized()` then degrades to empty results if a store is nil. WCDB row types under `Internal/WCDB/` never escape the package — they map to public immutable value models under `Models/` via `init(from:)`/`toModel()`, honoring the repo rule that app code only sees model types. `Storage/LibraryPaths` owns the whole directory layout (Documents/OfflineLibrary base excluded from backup, Caches/OfflineLibrary/LibraryArtwork for purgeable artwork) plus one-time migrations from Application Support and legacy cache directory names. `RuntimeDependencies` injects host-app closures (inspectAudioFile, resolveDownloadURL, fetchLyrics, fetchArtworkData, requestHeaders, setScreenAwake) so the package performs no networking itself; `DatabaseManager+Audit` produces an `AuditSnapshot` of counts, orphans, invalid paths, temp files, and schema mismatches.

SubsonicClientKit is a self-contained, dependency-free SwiftPM package (swift-tools 6.2, iOS 15/macOS 12) that implements the Subsonic REST API (v1.16.1, client name "museamp") behind one central type: `SubsonicMusicService`, a Sendable final class offering async `ping()`, `search()`, `album(id:)`, `song(id:)`, `playback(id:)`, and `lyrics(id:)`. Every call funnels through a single generic `perform(_:cache:decode:)` pipeline that builds URLs via the `SubsonicEndpoint` enum (which owns action names, query items, and per-endpoint `cacheIdentifier` strings), unwraps the `subsonic-response` JSON envelope, and maps raw `Subsonic*` DTOs (Models/Subsonic/, with lossy string/int decoding for inconsistent servers) into Apple-Music-shaped `Catalog*` public models (Models/, with attributes/relationships split per file). State ownership: the service owns five per-type in-memory `ResponseCache` actors (TTL hardcoded to infinity, 7-day stale window for failure fallback), a `RequestCoalescer` actor that dedupes concurrent identical requests, an NSLock-guarded `authMode` that auto-falls back from MD5-token auth to plain-password auth on auth-shaped failures, and an optional injected `CacheStorageProvider` for versioned raw-Data disk caching (currently nil in the app — only package tests exercise it). Authentication credentials are embedded as query parameters in every URL the package emits, including artwork template URL strings stored inside Catalog models and `PlaybackInfo.playbackURL`, which then cross into the app's Backend layers. Song-type searches optionally fan out per-song getSong enrichment via a task group, silently falling back to the thinner row on error. The only app-side consumer is `MuseAmp/Backend/API/APIClient.swift` (plus tvOS bootstrap), which constructs and rebuilds the service on configuration change; the package itself does no logging and no UIKit, communicating failures purely through the localized `APIError` enum.

MuseAmpPlayerKit is a self-contained Swift 6 (strict concurrency, swiftLanguageModes [.v6]) SwiftPM package providing queue-based audio playback with gapless preloading, lock-screen/Control Center integration, and a delegate-based API; its only dependency is Kingfisher (artwork). The central type is `MusicPlayer`, a `@MainActor final class` in Player/MusicPlayer.swift, split across responsibility extensions (+Playback transport and loadAndPlay/continueWithCurrentEngineItem, +Queue public queue editing, +Observation KVO/notification handling and item-failure advance, +Restoration restorePlayback, +Logging describe helpers). Queue truth lives in `PlaybackQueue` (Queue/PlaybackQueue.swift), a value-type struct owning the canonical `items` array, `currentIndex` (an index into the derived `effectiveOrder`, not into `items`), `playedIndices` history, and a `shufflePermutation`; consumers only ever see immutable `QueueSnapshot` values. The AVFoundation layer is abstracted behind the internal `AudioPlaybackEngine` protocol, implemented by `AVPlayerEngine` wrapping an `AVQueuePlayer` plus a tracked `preloadedItem` for gapless transitions (tests inject MockAudioPlaybackEngine through the internal init). MediaCenter/ holds three collaborators owned by MusicPlayer: `MediaCenterCoordinator` (selects `SessionMediaCenterBackend` using MPNowPlayingSession on iOS 16+/tvOS 14+ versus `LegacyMediaCenterBackend` using MPNowPlayingInfoCenter), `NowPlayingInfoManager` (mutates a `currentInfo` dictionary mirror and republishes it wholesale, including a 10-second auto-expiring lyric subtitle and async Kingfisher artwork via `ArtworkLoader`), and `RemoteCommandManager` (wires MPRemoteCommandCenter targets to a weak player reference). `AudioSessionManager` configures AVAudioSession (long-form audio policy with fallback) and routes interruption/route-change notifications back into pause/play. Models/ contains the small public Sendable value types (`PlayerItem`, `PlaybackState`, `RepeatMode`, `TransitionReason`, `QueueSnapshot`), and Logging/ defines the injected `MusicPlayerLogger` protocol with a `NoopMusicPlayerLogger` default. Nothing is persisted inside the package; session persistence and URL resolution live in the app's `MuseAmp/Backend/Playback/` (PlaybackController, PlaybackSessionStore), which is the package's primary consumer along with the MuseAmpTV shell.
